title: Notifying Family and Close Friends
summary: Deciding who to call, and in what order, can feel surprisingly hard in the middle of grief — a simple approach helps.
category: Death of a Loved One (Transition into After-a-Loss)
journeyStage: Death of a Loved One (Transition into After-a-Loss)
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Notifying Family and Close Friends | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Deciding who to call, and in what order, can feel surprisingly hard in the middle of grief — a simple approach helps.
whatYouNeedToKnow: A short list of 2–3 people to call first is enough — they can help notify others.
Phone calls (not texts) are generally better for the closest relationships.
It's completely fine to ask someone else to help make calls.

paragraphs: Rather than trying to personally reach everyone, identify 2–3 of the closest people first, and lean on them to help spread the word further — a sibling, close friend, or adult child can call others so the burden doesn't fall on one person.

Phone calls tend to feel more appropriate than texts for close relationships, though texting is reasonable for wider circles. There's no obligation to be the one making every call — asking someone else to help is completely reasonable.

nextSteps: Make a short list of who to call personally versus who others can notify.
Ask one person to help share the news further.
Give yourself permission to pause between calls.
disclaimer: However you choose to share this news is the right way — there's no single correct order or method.
